reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The command overview table at the top is excellent — a first-time reader gets the full picture in one glance before committing to any section.
Consistent H2/H3 rhythm across all 12 commands makes the page easy to skim: each command section has the same shape (intro, basic example, flags table or list).
Tables for DynamoDB, Redis, and OpenSearch operations are highly scannable and cite required flags per operation — exactly what a developer needs mid-task.
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service behavior, comparison questions) and is non-redundant across 10 entries.
The 'debug:container-exec vs container:session' FAQ entry gives a clear, opinionated answer to a question developers will actually ask.
Agent mode section is well-placed: grounded in the source code's isAgentMode() JSON path and shows the exact JSON shape, helping developers who use AI tooling.
Shell examples are consistently one command per fenced block with explanatory prose above each — the copy-button trap is fully avoided.
Relative time syntax (1h, 30m, 1d, 5s) is documented explicitly with matching examples; this is sourced correctly from debug-logs/index.ts.
Shared flags section near the top avoids repeating --projectName/--stage/--region in every command description.
problems: The opening sentence states 'All debug commands are read-only and do not modify your stack.' This is directly contradicted by container:session and bastion:session, which give interactive shell access capable of any modification. The claim should be scoped only to the debug:* commands.

The FAQ answer for 'What permissions do debug commands need?' describes 'a dedicated debug agent IAM role provisioned with your stack' — this specific claim is not grounded in either provided source file (debug-logs/index.ts and debug-metrics/index.ts both use awsSdkManager with standard credentials; neither provisions a dedicated role). This is likely fabricated and could mislead users debugging auth failures.

The claim that 'Stacktape configures ECS Exec automatically for deployed container workloads' in the container commands Info callout is not present in the provided source files. If inaccurate, a user following the docs will hit a confusing ECS Exec not enabled error.

Only 2 of the roughly 12 documented commands have source files provided (debug:logs and debug:metrics). The page asserts detailed behavior (specific SQL prefix allowlist, bastion:tunnel target type list, debug:alarms --state flag, OpenSearch operation list, debug:aws-sdk prefix allowlist) for commands whose source is unverifiable. If any of these are wrong, the page actively misleads users.
The 'Supported resource types and metrics' H3 heading is notably long compared to the otherwise tight heading discipline — not a mandatory flag, but inconsistent.
mandatoryFixes: Correct the opening paragraph's read-only claim. Replace 'All debug commands are read-only and do not modify your stack' with a scoped statement such as: 'The debug:* commands are read-only by design — they do not modify resources. The container:session and bastion:session commands open interactive sessions where you can run arbitrary commands, so treat them with appropriate care.'

Remove or rewrite the FAQ answer about IAM permissions. The 'dedicated debug agent IAM role provisioned with your stack' claim has no grounding in the provided source files. Replace with a conservative, source-grounded statement: debug commands use your configured AWS credentials (via awsSdkManager) and require read-only permissions on the relevant AWS services (CloudWatch, RDS Data API, etc.). If a dedicated role mechanism exists but was not in the provided source, the writer must verify it before publishing — do not ship a fabricated authorization model description.
optionalImprovements: Add a short callout or note near container:session and bastion:session clarifying that these require the bastion or ECS Exec feature to be configured in the stack — a first-time user encountering 'target not found' or 'ECS Exec not enabled' errors has no hint of what to check.

The 'debug:sql VPC-only databases' sub-section could mention that --bastionResource is only needed for databases without publicAccessibility enabled, so users with publicly accessible (dev/test) databases don't think a bastion is always required.

The 'debug:aws-sdk' Warning callout listing every supported service is verbose and better suited as a collapsed reference or link. For a first-time user, the important signal is 'read-only AWS SDK calls' — the full service list is noise at reading time.

Consider adding a short 'Prerequisites' note near the top (after Shared flags) clarifying that debug commands require a deployed stack and valid AWS credentials — this prevents the single most common support question from first-time users who run commands before deploying.

The 'CloudWatch Logs Insights queries' H3 is a niche feature (power users only). Add one sentence flagging the audience: 'Most users won't need this — use --filter for simple keyword matching. Logs Insights is worth learning when you need aggregations or field extraction across high-volume logs.'

The agent mode section documents the JSON shape for debug:logs only. A brief note that all debug:* commands switch to JSON in agent mode (or link to MCP docs) would complete the picture without padding.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Command overview table is well-constructed — purpose + target resource types in one scannable view; a production engineer can orient immediately.
Per-command operation tables (DynamoDB, Redis, OpenSearch) effectively replace wall-of-text flag lists with structured decision support.
Each bash code block contains exactly one command with prose explanation before it — no multi-command-in-one-block violations.
Agent mode / JSON output section is accurately grounded: source confirms `isAgentMode()` branching and the JSON shape shown matches the source code's output path.
FAQ coverage is broad and mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS-behavior, and comparison questions correctly. The bastion FAQ answers are practically useful.
Time range relative syntax (1h, 30m, 1d, 5s) correctly reflects the source regex `^(\d+)([hmds])$` including the `s` unit.
Default limit of 100 for debug:logs and defaults for period (300) and stat (Average) for debug:metrics both match source exactly.
problems: FACTUAL INACCURACY — Opening paragraph states 'All debug commands are read-only and do not modify your stack.' This is directly contradicted by debug:container-exec and container:session, both of which execute arbitrary commands inside running containers and can write files, mutate state, restart processes, or worse. An engineer reading this page in production may grant or accept a broader blast-radius than they realize.

UNVERIFIED DEFAULTS AND BEHAVIOR — The source files cover only debug:logs and debug:metrics. Details for debug:sql (limit=1000, SSL rejectUnauthorized:false, allowed statement prefixes), debug:dynamodb (operation list, pk/sk JSON format), debug:redis (operation list, --section flag), debug:opensearch (operation list), debug:aws-sdk (allowed prefix list, full service list), bastion:tunnel (supported target types), and container:session flags are not grounded in any provided source. If any of these are fabricated, a production engineer following them will hit unexpected errors.

UNVERIFIED FAQ CLAIM — 'What permissions do debug commands need?' describes a 'dedicated debug agent IAM role provisioned with your stack' with explicit fallback behavior. No source file supports this claim. If the IAM model is different (e.g., commands use the caller's configured AWS credentials directly), engineers will be confused when permissions fail.

debug:metrics output description ('sparkline visualization along with min, max, average, and sum statistics') is unverifiable — source only imports printMetricsChart without showing its implementation. If the terminal output is actually different (e.g., plain tabular data), engineers may expect a visual they won't get.

The --stat flag for debug:metrics lists five valid values (Average, Sum, Minimum, Maximum, SampleCount). Source shows `stat = 'Average'` as default but does not enumerate or validate the accepted stat values in the provided excerpt. These may be correct CloudWatch stat names, but claiming them as the exhaustive supported set is unsourced.
mandatoryFixes: Correct the opening claim 'All debug commands are read-only and do not modify your stack.' The observability commands (debug:logs, debug:metrics, debug:alarms, debug:sql, debug:dynamodb, debug:redis, debug:opensearch, debug:aws-sdk) are read-only. debug:container-exec and container:session are explicitly not — they execute arbitrary shell commands inside live containers. The page must distinguish these two groups explicitly, not blanket-label all commands as safe. Suggested rewrite: 'Stacktape observability commands (debug:logs, debug:metrics, debug:alarms, debug:sql, debug:dynamodb, debug:redis, debug:opensearch, debug:aws-sdk) are read-only. The container execution commands (debug:container-exec, container:session) and bastion commands let you interact with live infrastructure and are not restricted to read-only operations — use them with care in production stages.'
optionalImprovements: Add a note next to commands whose details could not be verified from source (debug:sql, debug:dynamodb, debug:redis, debug:opensearch, debug:aws-sdk, bastion:tunnel, bastion:session) that these flag lists are based on CLI help output, so engineers know to run `stacktape <command> --help` to confirm current defaults.

The --bastionResource note for debug:redis says 'typically required' — for a VPC-only ElastiCache Redis cluster (the Stacktape default), it is always required. Hedging with 'typically' may cause engineers to skip adding the bastion and then hit a connection refusal. Harden the wording: 'Required for VPC-private Redis clusters, which is the Stacktape default.'

The debug:metrics section could clarify that passing an unsupported metric name (one not in the allowed list for the resource type) produces a CLI error that includes the available metrics — this is grounded in the source's validation path (`metricConfig.metrics.includes(metric)` check) and helps engineers troubleshoot faster.

The 'What permissions do debug commands need?' FAQ should either be removed or rewritten to say engineers should consult the deployed IAM roles in the AWS Console or CloudFormation stack outputs, since the 'debug agent IAM role' claim is unverifiable from source and could mislead engineers when troubleshooting permission errors.

The debug:metrics sparkline and statistics description should be qualified with 'in supported terminals' or similar, or softened to 'chart visualization' rather than 'sparkline chart' if the actual terminal rendering cannot be confirmed from source.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page is easy to scan: command overview table, short H2s, per-command sections, and FAQ structure all help a busy developer find commands quickly.

The `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics` sections include several source-grounded details: required `--resourceName`, `--metric`, default log limit `100`, default metric period `300`, default statistic `Average`, relative time parsing, Logs Insights `--query`, and metrics supported by the four resource types in the source.
Shell snippets follow the one-command-per-block rule and do not contain explanatory `#` comments inside command blocks.
problems: The draft makes many Stacktape-specific claims that are not grounded in the provided source files. Only `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics` source files were provided, but the page documents `debug:alarms`, `debug:sql`, `debug:dynamodb`, `debug:redis`, `debug:opensearch`, `debug:aws-sdk`, `debug:container-exec`, `container:session`, `bastion:tunnel`, and `bastion:session` in detail.

The opening paragraph is self-contained and in the right length range, but it overclaims: “Every command,” “All debug commands are read-only,” and the shared flag requirements are not supported by the provided files and are especially risky for `container:session` and container command execution.

The page incorrectly presents `--endTime` as applying to normal `debug:logs` fetching. In the provided `debug-logs` source, `endTime` is used for Logs Insights queries, while standard `getLogEvents` receives `startTime` only.

Several `debug:logs` output claims are unsupported by the provided source, including supported target resource types, severity color mapping details, summary statistics, and the statement that CloudWatch Logs filter syntax is standard.

The `debug:metrics` section is partly grounded, but “sparkline chart,” “min, max, average, and sum statistics,” and “web services, private services, and worker services are container workloads under the hood” are not established by the provided source files.

The “Shared flags” section is too broad. The provided command implementations require `--resourceName` for both commands and `--metric` for metrics, but they do not prove that every debug command accepts the same four flags.

The FAQ contains useful questions, but many answers rely on unsupported behavior: live log streaming via dev mode, debug commands working with dev mode stacks, debug agent IAM role behavior, credential fallback, piping behavior beyond `debug:logs --raw`, and cost claims.

The page includes deployed-stack and dev-mode claims, but the provided sources only show `commandRequiresDeployedStack: true` for logs and deployed-stack initialization for metrics. The dev mode relationship is not grounded enough for this local-development page’s requirement to separate dev-mode behavior from deployed-stack behavior.
The “Command overview” table is useful for readers but mostly unsupported by the source list. It should be limited to sourced commands or backed by additional CLI source files.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or clearly defer detailed sections for commands whose source files were not provided, or add the corresponding CLI source files and re-ground each command section from those files.
Correct the `debug:logs --endTime` wording: describe `--endTime` only for Logs Insights `--query` unless additional source proves standard log fetching uses it.

Replace broad statements like “All debug commands are read-only” and “Every command accepts these standard flags” with source-grounded wording limited to `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics`, or provide source for every listed command.

Rewrite the FAQ so every Stacktape-specific answer is grounded in the provided files. Remove unsupported claims about dev mode stacks, debug agent IAM roles, credential fallback, container sessions, bastion behavior, and AWS charges unless source files are added.

For this local-development page, explicitly separate what the provided sources prove about deployed-stack behavior from any dev-mode behavior. With the current sources, say `debug:logs` requires a deployed stack and avoid claiming dev-mode parity.
optionalImprovements: Split the page into a narrow `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics` reference if only those source files are available; that would make the page much more accurate and still useful.
Keep the command overview table, but add a “Covered on this page” scope note if only some commands are source-grounded.
Add a small comparison table for `--filter` versus `--query` in `debug:logs`; both are grounded in the source and help readers choose the right mode.

In the metrics section, keep the supported resource type and metric table because it maps directly to `METRIC_CONFIGS`, but avoid describing output formatting beyond JSON agent mode unless `printMetricsChart` source is included.
Use cautious wording such as “`debug:metrics` supports these resource types in the provided command implementation” instead of generalizing to all Stacktape resources.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is only partially grounded in the provided sources. The `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics` sections contain several correctly sourced details, but the page makes broad claims about many other CLI commands for which no source files were provided. reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

I found several concrete issues in the sourced areas too, especially around `debug:logs` output and unsupported resource-type claims.

issues.statement: “The command supports Lambda functions, container workloads (web services, private services, worker services, multi-container workloads), and batch jobs — any resource that produces a CloudWatch log group.”
issues.reason: The `debug:logs` implementation delegates resource support to `getLogGroupInfoForStacktapeResource`, but that helper is not included in the provided source. The visible command source does not enumerate Lambda functions, web services, private services, worker services, multi-container workloads, or batch jobs, and it does not establish the broad rule “any resource that produces a CloudWatch log group.”
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to what the visible source proves: `debug:logs` requires `--resourceName`, resolves that resource’s log group through Stacktape’s log-group helper, and fetches CloudWatch Logs events. Avoid listing supported resource types unless the helper source is included.

issues.statement: “For standard log fetching, events are retrieved from `startTime` forward. For Logs Insights queries (using `--query`), both `--startTime` and `--endTime` bound the query window explicitly.”
issues.reason: The Logs Insights part is grounded, but the standard log-fetching sentence omits that `endTime` is not passed to `getLogEvents`. In the visible source, standard log fetching passes `startTime` only, while Logs Insights passes both `startTime` and `endTime`. The draft should explicitly state that `--endTime` affects relative `--startTime` calculation for standard fetching but is not used as a CloudWatch end bound there.
issues.suggestedFix: Write: “For standard log fetching, Stacktape passes `startTime` to CloudWatch Logs. `--endTime` is used as the reference point when parsing relative `--startTime`; Logs Insights queries pass both `startTime` and `endTime`.”

issues.statement: “Without `--raw`, Stacktape prints formatted log output in the terminal with color-coding by severity: red for `ERROR`/`FATAL`/`CRITICAL`, yellow for `WARN`, and gray for `DEBUG`. A summary line shows counts by level.”
issues.reason: The command delegates formatting to `printFormattedLogs`, but the formatter implementation is not in the provided source files. The visible source does not prove severity color mapping or a summary line with level counts.
issues.suggestedFix: Limit the statement to “Without `--raw`, Stacktape passes the limited events to the formatted log printer.” If formatter source is added, document exact colors and summary behavior from that file.

issues.statement: “Stacktape provides a suite of CLI commands… The observability commands (`debug:alarms`, `debug:sql`, `debug:dynamodb`, `debug:redis`, `debug:opensearch`, `debug:aws-sdk`) are read-only…” plus the sections documenting those commands, container execution, bastion tunneling, credentials, and FAQ behavior.
issues.reason: Only `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics` source files are provided. The draft documents many other commands with specific flags, defaults, resource support, read-only enforcement, role-assumption behavior, and fallback behavior that cannot be verified from the supplied sources. Several of these claims would directly affect how readers run production commands.
issues.suggestedFix: Either provide the command source files for the remaining CLI commands or remove/narrow those sections to links only. For this source set, the page can confidently document `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics`, then link to the other CLI reference pages without asserting their internals.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.

debug:logs section is thorough: time range options, two filtering variants shown in separate blocks, Logs Insights query path, output flags (--raw/--limit/--container), and agent mode JSON schema all covered with concrete examples

debug:metrics section includes the supported-resource-types table with CloudWatch namespace and available metrics per type — this is the highest-signal information for that command and saves a round-trip to CLI reference
Warning callout for non-read-only container/bastion commands is correctly placed and appropriately scoped
Dev-mode vs deployed-stack distinction is handled explicitly ('All commands require a deployed stack... not just dev mode stacks') in compliance with section instructions
FAQ mixes Stacktape-specific questions, AWS-service questions (cost, read-only SQL), and comparison questions (exec vs session), which is the right coverage pattern
No mandatory style violations: no hand-authored YAML config, no StacktapeConfig type pattern, no defineConfig in fenced TypeScript blocks, no multi-command bash blocks
problems: Nine of twelve commands receive only a one-sentence description plus a 'See the CLI reference' link — no working example, no flags, no tradeoffs. The source llm-docs file demonstrates that full flag tables, usage examples, and operation lists exist for debug:sql, debug:dynamodb, debug:redis, debug:opensearch, debug:aws-sdk, debug:container-exec, container:session, bastion:tunnel, and bastion:session. A developer arriving at this page with a broken Redis cluster or DynamoDB query finds nothing actionable.

debug:alarms section has zero usage examples and no flag documentation. The llm-docs source shows the command supports --state filtering (ALARM/OK/INSUFFICIENT_DATA) and --resourceName scoping — both are high-value for a developer triage workflow.

The source llm-docs covers the debug agent role, when it applies, fallback behavior, and SSM-tunneled commands — all relevant context a developer encounters when a debug command fails with a permissions error.

bastion:tunnel supported resource types are not mentioned — the source llm-docs enumerates relational-database, redis-cluster, application-load-balancer, private-service. A developer with a mongo-atlas or kinesis-stream will hit an error; knowing up front which types are supported prevents wasted troubleshooting.
optionalImprovements: Add a basic usage example and a condensed flags table to each thin section (debug:sql, debug:dynamodb, debug:redis, debug:opensearch, debug:aws-sdk, debug:container-exec, container:session, bastion:tunnel, bastion:session). debug:alarms: add the basic usage example (no --resourceName required), a filter-by-state example using --state ALARM, and the note that it scans alarms by stack-name prefix. Without these, the section is a stub.

Credentials and permissions: replace the 'see each CLI reference page' cop-out with the actual behavior from the source — the debug agent role stored as DebugAgentRoleArn, which commands attempt role assumption (debug:dynamodb, debug:opensearch, debug:aws-sdk), the fallback to configured credentials, and the recommendation to redeploy to provision the role.

bastion:tunnel: add the supported resource types list (relational-database, redis-cluster, application-load-balancer, private-service) so developers know immediately whether their resource can be tunneled before attempting the command.

debug:container-exec: clarify that it targets ECS workloads specifically (web services, private services, worker services, multi-container workloads) — Lambda functions are not supported and a developer unfamiliar with ECS will not know this distinction.

The intro accurately warns 'treat them with appropriate care in production stages' for container/bastion commands but conflates bastion:session (SSM shell on EC2) with debug:container-exec/container:session (shell inside a container). reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The opening paragraph is self-contained, within the target length, and clearly distinguishes deployed-stack debugging from dev mode behavior.
The command overview table is highly scannable and gives readers a fast map of the available CLI surface.
The `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics` sections are concrete, with supported flags, defaults, examples, and output behavior grounded in the supplied command sources.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule and avoid explanatory `#` comments inside terminal snippets.
The FAQ covers Stacktape-specific usage, underlying AWS cost mechanics, and choosing between related commands.
problems: The first paragraph slightly blurs command behavior: `debug:container-exec` runs a one-shot command, but the sentence groups it with commands that open interactive sessions.

Several H2 sections after `Viewing alarms` are thin link stubs. They are scannable, but not self-contained enough for AI extraction because the first paragraph often says only to see another CLI reference page.

The `Stack targeting` section claims every command identifies the target stack using `--projectName`, `--stage`, and `--region`; the supplied source shows stack initialization but does not prove those exact flags for every listed command.

The page states detailed credential and IAM behavior for all debug commands even though only `debug:logs`, `debug:metrics`, and the generated summary are provided. The wording should be narrower unless command-specific sources are added.

The FAQ includes a concrete CloudWatch Logs Insights price of `~$0.005/GB scanned` that is not present in the supplied source files; pricing rules require concrete pricing only when present in source.
Some FAQ answers refer to earlier tables or sections, such as “listed in the metrics table above,” which weakens extractability when the paragraph is cited alone.
mandatoryFixes: Revise the opening paragraph so `debug:container-exec` is described as a one-shot container command and only `container:session` / `bastion:session` are described as interactive shell sessions.
Remove or source the concrete CloudWatch Logs Insights price. If no pricing source is provided, say AWS charges for Logs Insights queries based on data scanned without quoting a dollar amount.

Narrow the `Stack targeting` claim to avoid saying every command uses `--projectName`, `--stage`, and `--region` unless the command sources prove it. For example: “Debug commands target a deployed stack through the usual Stacktape stack-selection flags; resource-specific commands also require `--resourceName`.”
optionalImprovements: Make each thin command section more extractable by adding one concrete sentence about when to use the command and one caution or limitation, instead of only linking to the CLI reference.
Turn the read-only versus live-interaction distinction into a small table near the top so readers can quickly identify safe inspection commands versus commands that execute inside infrastructure.
In FAQ answers, repeat the relevant command names and supported values instead of relying on “above” references.
Consider adding a short “Dev mode vs deployed stacks” section because the route is under local development and the human-maintained instruction explicitly asks to separate those behaviors.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly grounded for `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics`, which are covered by the provided CLI source. The main issues are over-broad wording for container execution, an unsupported blanket claim about stack-targeting flags, and an over-specific credentials/IAM section not supported by the provided command sources.

issues.statement: The container execution commands (`debug:container-exec`, `container:session`) and the bastion shell command (`bastion:session`) open interactive sessions where you can run arbitrary commands inside live infrastructure.
issues.reason: The statement incorrectly groups `debug:container-exec` with interactive session commands. The provided generated source describes `debug:container-exec` as a one-shot command, while `container:session` is the interactive shell command.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite to distinguish the commands: `debug:container-exec` runs a one-shot command in a container, while `container:session` and `bastion:session` open interactive sessions.

Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.

The source llm-docs file demonstrates that full flag tables, usage examples, and operation lists exist for debug:sql, debug:dynamodb, debug:redis, debug:opensearch, debug:aws-sdk, debug:container-exec, container:session, bastion:tunnel, and bastion:session. A developer arriving at this page with a broken Redis cluster or DynamoDB query finds nothing actionable.

debug:alarms section has zero usage examples and no flag documentation. The llm-docs source shows the command supports --state filtering (ALARM/OK/INSUFFICIENT_DATA) and --resourceName scoping — both are high-value for a developer triage workflow.

bastion:tunnel: add the supported resource types list (relational-database, redis-cluster, application-load-balancer, private-service) so developers know immediately whether their resource can be tunneled before attempting the command.

debug:container-exec: clarify that it targets ECS workloads specifically (web services, private services, worker services, multi-container workloads) — Lambda functions are not supported and a developer unfamiliar with ECS will not know this distinction.

The intro accurately warns 'treat them with appropriate care in production stages' for container/bastion commands but conflates bastion:session (SSM shell on EC2) with debug:container-exec/container:session (shell inside a container). reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The opening paragraph is self-contained, within the target length, and clearly distinguishes deployed-stack debugging from dev mode behavior.
The command overview table is highly scannable and gives readers a fast map of the available CLI surface.
The `debug:logs` and `debug:metrics` sections are concrete, with supported flags, defaults, examples, and output behavior grounded in the supplied command sources.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule and avoid explanatory `#` comments inside terminal snippets.
The FAQ covers Stacktape-specific usage, underlying AWS cost mechanics, and choosing between related commands.
problems: The first paragraph slightly blurs command behavior: `debug:container-exec` runs a one-shot command, but the sentence groups it with commands that open interactive sessions.

Several H2 sections after `Viewing alarms` are thin link stubs. They are scannable, but not self-contained enough for AI extraction because the first paragraph often says only to see another CLI reference page.

The `Stack targeting` section claims every command identifies the target stack using `--projectName`, `--stage`, and `--region`; the supplied source shows stack initialization but does not prove those exact flags for every listed command.

The page states detailed credential and IAM behavior for all debug commands even though only `debug:logs`, `debug:metrics`, and the generated summary are provided. The wording should be narrower unless command-specific sources are added.

The FAQ includes a concrete CloudWatch Logs Insights price of `~$0.005/GB scanned` that is not present in the supplied source files; pricing rules require concrete pricing only when present in source.
Some FAQ answers refer to earlier tables or sections, such as “listed in the metrics table above,” which weakens extractability when the paragraph is cited alone.
mandatoryFixes: Revise the opening paragraph so `debug:container-exec` is described as a one-shot container command and only `container:session` / `bastion:session` are described as interactive shell sessions.
Remove or source the concrete CloudWatch Logs Insights price. If no pricing source is provided, say AWS charges for Logs Insights queries based on data scanned without quoting a dollar amount.

Narrow the `Stack targeting` claim to avoid saying every command uses `--projectName`, `--stage`, and `--region` unless the command sources prove it. For example: “Debug commands target a deployed stack through the usual Stacktape stack-selection flags; resource-specific commands also require `--resourceName`.”
optionalImprovements: Make each thin command section more extractable by adding one concrete sentence about when to use the command and one caution or limitation, instead of only linking to the CLI reference.
Turn the read-only versus live-interaction distinction into a small table near the top so readers can quickly identify safe inspection commands versus commands that execute inside infrastructure.
In FAQ answers, repeat the relevant command names and supported values instead of relying on “above” references.
Consider adding a short “Dev mode vs deployed stacks” section because the route is under local development and the human-maintained instruction explicitly asks to separate those behaviors.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly consistent with the provided command source and generated debug-command source. I found one credential wording issue that could mislead readers about when Stacktape uses configured AWS credentials directly versus assuming a stack-provisioned debug role.

issues.statement: “Debug commands use your configured AWS credentials to talk to AWS APIs and SSM.”
issues.reason: The generated source says some debug commands first attempt to assume a dedicated debug agent IAM role and only fall back to configured AWS credentials if that role is unavailable or assumption fails. The draft mentions stack-provisioned roles later in the sentence, but the opening sentence is too broad and can read as if configured credentials are always the credentials used for AWS API calls.
issues.suggestedFix: Write narrowly: “Debug commands start from your configured AWS credentials. Some inspection commands may assume a stack-provisioned debug agent role; commands that access VPC resources or ECS/SSM sessions use AWS credentials to establish those sessions.”

evidence.quote: Debug commands that query AWS services (`debug:dynamodb`, `debug:opensearch`, `debug:aws-sdk`) attempt to assume a dedicated debug agent IAM role provisioned with your stack. This role is stored as `DebugAgentRoleArn` in the stack's custom outputs and scoped to the stack's resources. If the role isn't available or assumption fails (e.g., the stack was deployed before debug roles were introduced), commands fall back to your configured AWS credentials.
